perturbation.original_text: 5.2 CPDC Limited Product Warranty. CPDC hereby provides a limited product warranty, and accordingly does warrant for each Batch, that the Product shipped will (i) conform with the Specifications, (ii) be manufactured, tested, processed, packed and prepared for shipment in accordance with cGMPs, and (iii) be free from defects in material and workmanship for the period from the date of manufacture to the expiry date set out on each Unit of Product packed and prepared for shipment.
perturbation.changed_text: 5.2 CPDC Limited Product Warranty. CPDC hereby provides a limited product warranty, and accordingly does warrant for each Batch, that the Product shipped will (i) conform with the Specifications, and (ii) be manufactured, tested, processed, packed and prepared for shipment in accordance with cGMPs.
perturbation.explanation: By removing the warranty of being free from defects in material and workmanship, the contract now omits a standard warranty, violating consumer protection laws that imply such warranties, especially concerning pharmaceutical products.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Uniform Commercial Code - Implied Warranty of Merchantability
perturbation.law_explanation: The modified text removes the warranty against defects in material and workmanship, which directly contradicts the implied warranty of merchantability under UCC § 2-314. This section of the UCC states that goods must be 'free from any defect not generally acceptable in the trade' to be considered merchantable.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: § 2-314. Implied Warranty: Merchantability; Usage of Trade. | Uniform Commercial Code | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you § 2-314. Implied Warranty: Merchantability; Usage of Trade. (1) Unless excluded or modified (Section 2-316 ), a warranty that the goods shall be merchantable is implied in a contract for their sale if the seller is a merchant with respect to goods of that kind. Under this section the serving for value of food or drink to be consumed either on the premises or elsewhere is a sale. (2) Goods to be merchantable must be at least such as (a) pass without objection in the trade under the contract description; and (b) in the case of fungible goods , are of fair average quality within the description; and (c) are fit for the ordinary purposes for which such goods are used; and (d) run, within the variations permitted by the agreement , of even kind, quality and quantity within each unit and among all units involved; and (e) are adequately contained, packaged, and labeled as the agreement may require; and (f) conform to the promise or affirmations of fact made on the container or label if any. (3) Unless excluded or modified (Section 2-316 ) other implied warranties may arise from course of dealing or usage of trade. Uniform Commercial Code Toolbox About Uniform Laws State Uniform Commercial Codes
perturbation.justification: The modified text removes a warranty against defects in material and workmanship. The Law Explanation states this contradicts the implied warranty of merchantability under UCC § 2-314. Scraped Law Snippet 1, referencing UCC § 2-314, states a warranty that goods shall be merchantable is implied in a contract. It also specifies that goods to be merchantable must be free from any defect. By removing the 'free from defects' warranty, the modified contract contradicts the UCC § 2-314 implied warranty of merchantability, creating a legal contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: 3.6 Batch Testing. FUSION shall have the right but not the obligation to conduct any Batch testing [***] or investigation it determines to be of value to determine compliance of Product with the Specifications and/or pursuant to any other standard imposed by law. A Batch shall be deemed to be acceptable if, upon testing, it meets the Specifications, relevant cGMP standards, all other applicable laws, rules and regulations (and any additional tests as agreed by the Parties). If either Party discovers that a Batch does not meet the Specifications, relevant cGMP standards or other applicable laws, rules or regulations, then the discovering Party shall promptly communicate with the other Party.
perturbation.changed_text: 3.6 Batch Testing. FUSION shall have the right but not the obligation to conduct any Batch testing [***] or investigation it determines to be of value to determine compliance of Product with the Specifications and/or pursuant to any other standard imposed by law. A Batch shall be deemed to be acceptable if, upon testing, it meets the Specifications. If either Party discovers that a Batch does not meet the Specifications, then the discovering Party shall promptly communicate with the other Party.
perturbation.explanation: The clause explicitly requires batch testing to adhere to relevant cGMP standards, all other applicable laws, rules and regulations. By removing 'relevant cGMP standards, all other applicable laws, rules and regulations' it weakens the commitment to comply with necessary external requirements, opening it up to legal challenges for non-compliance.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Current Good Manufacturing Practice (CGMP) regulations enforced by the FDA
perturbation.law_explanation: By removing the requirement for batches to meet relevant cGMP standards and applicable laws, the modified text contradicts 21 CFR Parts 210 and 211, which mandate adherence to CGMPs for drug manufacturing. These regulations ensure the safety, efficacy, and quality of drugs, and omitting the requirement for compliance exposes the manufacturer to legal and regulatory risks.

perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: Current Good Manufacturing Practice (CGMP) Regulations | FDA Skip to main content Skip to FDA Search Skip to in this section menu Skip to footer links In this section : Pharmaceutical Quality Resources Home Drugs Development & Approval Process | Drugs Pharmaceutical Quality Resources Current Good Manufacturing Practice (CGMP) Regulations Pharmaceutical Quality Resources May 2023: Immediately in Effect Guidance for Industry: Testing of Glycerin, Propylene Glycol, Maltitol Solution, Hydrogenated Starch Hydrolysate, Sorbitol Solution, and other High-Risk Drug Components for Diethylene Glycol and Ethylene Glycol FDA ensures the quality of drug products by carefully monitoring drug manufacturers' compliance with its Current Good Manufacturing Practice (CGMP) regulations. The CGMP regulations for drugs contain minimum requirements for the methods, facilities, and controls used in manufacturing, processing, and packing of a drug product. The regulations make sure that a product is safe for use, and that it has the ingredients and strength it claims to have. The approval process for new and generic drug marketing applications includes a review of the manufacturer's compliance with the CGMP. FDA assessors and investigators determine whether the firm has the necessary facilities, equipment, and ability to manufacture the drug it intends to market. Code of Federal Regulations (CFR) FDA's portion of the CFR is in Title 21, which interprets the Federal Food, Drug and Cosmetic Act and related statutes, including the Public Health Service Act. The pharmaceutical or drug quality-related regulations appear in several parts of Title 21, including sections in parts 1-99, 200-299, 300-499, 600-799, and 800-1299. The regulations enable a common understanding of the regulatory process by describing the requirements to be followed by drug manufacturers, applicants, and FDA. 21 CFR Part 314 For FDA approval to market a new drug. 21 CFR Part 210 . Current Good Manufacturing Practice in Manufacturing Processing, packing, or Holding of Drugs. 21 CFR Part 211 . Current Good Manufacturing Practice for Finished Pharmaceuticals. 21 CFR Part 212 . Current Good Manufacturing Practice for Positron Emission Tomography Drugs. 21 CFR Part 600 . Biological Products: General. The following pages provide more information with respect to regulations for particular product types and manufacturing considerations: Field Alert Reports (for NDAs and ANDAs) Biological Product Deviation Reports (for BLAs) Contact for Further Information: CDER-OPQ-Inquiries@fda.hhs.gov Content current as of: 01/21/2025 Back to Top
perturbation.justification: The changed text removes the requirement for batch testing to adhere to 'relevant cGMP standards, all other applicable laws, rules and regulations,' which directly contradicts the FDA's CGMP regulations. These regulations, as highlighted in Scraped Law Snippet 2, 'contain minimum requirements for the methods, facilities, and controls used in manufacturing, processing, and packing of a drug product' and ensure product safety and efficacy. The removal weakens the commitment to these external requirements, potentially leading to non-compliance, making this a significant legal contradiction, under the Omission Legal Contradiction definition, where 'a contract omits a legally mandated consumer protection, making it non-compliant'. Scraped Law Snippet 1 explicitly mentions 'Part 210 Current Good Manufacturing Practice in Manufacturing, Processing, Packing, or Holding of Drugs; General' and 'Part 211 Current Good Manufacturing Practice for Finished Pharmaceuticals,' further supporting the importance of adhering to cGMP standards.

perturbation.original_text: 4.6 Complaints and Adverse Reactions. CPDC or FUSION shall provide to each other prompt notice of any information either of them receives regarding the safety of the Precursor, reference standards, excipients, Products or isotopes, including any confirmed or unconfirmed information regarding adverse, serious or unexpected events associated with any Product that may implicate the manufacture of the Product or one of its components; provided, however, that FUSION shall not be required to provide Clinical Trial reporting to CPDC. For all complaints with respect to any Product of which a Party becomes aware concerning adverse reactions or safety issues, notice must be given by telephone within [***] after receipt of the information, followed immediately with written notice, advising the other Party, regardless of the origin of such information. Any other complaints shall be reported in writing to the other party [***]. CPDC agrees to co-operate with FUSION and any Regulatory Authority in evaluating any complaint, claim, safety or adverse use report related to any Product CPDC will provide timely assistance in responding to any such complaints, including reviews of Batch records and retained samples as well as any necessary testing within reason.
perturbation.changed_text: 4.6 Complaints and Adverse Reactions. CPDC or FUSION shall provide to each other notice of any information either of them receives regarding the safety of the Precursor, reference standards, excipients, Products or isotopes. For all complaints with respect to any Product of which a Party becomes aware concerning adverse reactions or safety issues, notice may be given at the party's discretion. Any other complaints shall be reported in writing to the other party [***]. CPDC agrees to co-operate with FUSION in evaluating any complaint, claim, safety or adverse use report related to any Product.
perturbation.explanation: By changing 'prompt notice' to just 'notice' and removing the requirement to report adverse reactions and safety issues 'by telephone within [***] after receipt of the information, followed immediately with written notice,' the revised clause weakens the reporting requirements. The clause moves to 'notice may be given at the party's discretion'. This violates regulations regarding the timely reporting of adverse events related to pharmaceutical products.
perturbation.contradicted_law: FDA Adverse Event Reporting System (FAERS) requirements
perturbation.law_explanation: By making adverse event reporting discretionary, the modified text contradicts 21 CFR 314.80, which mandates the prompt reporting of adverse drug experiences to the FDA. This regulation requires specific timelines and methods for reporting, and removing these requirements introduces significant legal risk.

perturbation.justification: The modified text contradicts the law by making adverse event reporting discretionary, whereas regulations such as 21 CFR 314.80 mandate prompt reporting. The original text required 'prompt notice' of safety information and immediate notification of adverse reactions, but the changed text states that 'notice may be given at the party's discretion.' This directly conflicts with the legal requirement for timely reporting, as supported by the Law Explanation's reference to 21 CFR 314.80. Scraped Law Snippet 1, referencing 21 CFR 314.80, further supports this requirement, although the snippet is incomplete. The change removes a mandatory obligation, creating a clear contradiction.
